Structured Learning Modules

Same language as file: English

High-Yield Clinical Comparison

ECG + Nursing Priorities
RhythmKey ECG ClueClinical SignificancePriority Nursing/Medical Response
First-degree AV blockPR interval > 0.20 sec; every impulse conducted; QRS usually normal.Usually asymptomatic and not serious.Monitor rhythm and treat associated conditions.
Mobitz I / WenckebachProgressive PR lengthening until a QRS drops; grouped beats.Often transient, but may warn of complete block in acute MI.Observe if asymptomatic; atropine/temporary pacing for symptomatic patients; TCP standby.
Mobitz IIConstant PR on conducted beats with dropped QRS; QRS often >0.12 sec.Poor prognosis; may progress to third-degree AV block and decrease CO.Prepare for pacing; permanent pacemaker indicated.
Third-degree AV blockNo relationship between P waves and QRS complexes; AV dissociation.Reduced CO, ischemia, HF, shock, syncope, possible asystole.TCP → temporary transvenous pacemaker; dopamine/epinephrine interim; permanent pacemaker; atropine ineffective.
PVCsEarly, wide, distorted QRS >0.12 sec; P wave usually hidden; compensatory pause may occur.Often benign in normal heart; significant in CAD/MI due to ventricular irritability.Assess hemodynamics and pulse deficit; correct hypoxia/electrolytes; beta-blocker/lidocaine/amiodarone when needed.
VT3+ PVCs; ventricular rate 150–250/min; wide distorted QRS; P often buried.Life-threatening; may progress to VF; can cause hypotension, pulmonary edema, low cerebral blood flow, arrest.Stable: antiarrhythmics/cardioversion if ineffective. Pulseless: CPR + rapid defibrillation + epinephrine/amiodarone.
VFChaotic irregular waveform; no measurable HR, PR, or QRS.No effective contraction, no CO; unresponsive, pulseless, apneic.Immediate CPR/ACLS and defibrillation; no delay.
